title: Msemen
subtitle: Moroccan laminated flatbread with flaky, folded layers
excerpt: Msemen are square Moroccan flatbreads made by folding a buttered dough over itself to create thin, flaky layers, then cooking them in a dry pan. They are eaten at breakfast with honey and argan oil, or alongside a bowl of harira soup. The dough takes about an hour to rest; each flatbread cooks in 6 to 8 minutes.
sourceNotes: Msemen are documented across Morocco, Algeria and Tunisia, with regional variations in grain composition and folding technique. The laminated flatbread method appears in Claudia Roden's 'A New Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1985) and in Fatima Hal's 'The Food of Morocco' (2011). The combination of plain flour and fine semolina is traditional in most Moroccan home kitchens.

recipe.freezeNotes: Stack cooled msemen between sheets of baking paper and freeze for up to 2 months. Reheat in a dry pan for 2 minutes per side from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough balls can be prepared and refrigerated overnight. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before laminating and cooking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: pan-fried over medium heat; no oven required

glossaryTerms.definition: The folding technique that gives msemen its characteristic flaky, layered structure. Each ball of dough is stretched thin on an oiled surface, spread with softened butter and fine semolina, then folded into thirds in both directions to create a square packet. When the flatbread is cooked in a dry pan, the fat between the layers melts and separates them, creating a honeycomb of tender flakes. The key is stretching the dough as thin as possible before folding.

content.text: The texture of msemen depends on
: stretching the dough as thin as possible before folding. Oil your hands and the work surface generously. The dough should be transparent when held to the light before the first fold.

Use a well-seasoned cast-iron pan or heavy non-stick frying pan with no added fat. The flatbreads cook in their own laminated butter and should blister slightly in the pan without burning. Keep the heat at a steady medium throughout.

Combine the 200 g plain flour, 100 g semolina, 3 g yeast and 5 g salt. Pour in the 180 ml tepid water and mix to a smooth, soft dough. It should not be sticky. Knead for 5 minutes until elastic, then cover and leave for 45 minutes at room temperature.
Divide the dough into 8 equal pieces (about 60 g each). Roll each into a smooth ball. Oil a tray and place the balls on it. Cover and leave for 15 minutes to relax.

Oil the work surface and your hands. Take one ball and press it into a thin round, stretching gently from the centre outward until it is almost transparent, roughly 25 cm across. Spread a teaspoon of softened butter over the surface and dust lightly with fine semolina. Fold the right third over the centre, then the left third over that. Fold the top third down and the bottom third up to form a small square. Set aside and repeat with the remaining balls.

Heat a dry heavy frying pan over medium heat. Take a square of laminated dough and gently press it out to about 15 cm. Place in the dry pan and cook for 3 to 4 minutes until golden brown with darker blisters on the underside. Flip and cook for a further 3 to 4 minutes until golden on the second side, the edges are set and pulling away from the pan, and the layers are visibly separated. Cook the remaining msemen in batches. Serve warm.

items.problem: Layers do not separate when cooking
items.solution: The butter was not spread thinly enough, or the dough was not stretched thin enough before folding. The butter layer needs to be nearly paper-thin to melt and separate cleanly. Stretching the dough is more important than the amount of butter used.
The gluten needs more rest. Cover the stretched dough and leave for 5 minutes before continuing. The 15-minute rest after dividing is a minimum; a longer rest makes stretching easier.

The heat was too high. Medium heat is correct for msemen: the outside should colour slowly enough for the layers to cook through. Reduce the heat and cover the pan loosely for the last minute on each side if the centre remains doughy.
